title: Global thermodynamics for heat-conducting fluids under weak gravity
abstract: We study liquid-gas coexistence under gravity and heat conduction from the viewpoint of global thermodynamics. We construct a variational free-energy function for the fixed-global-temperature description and decompose it into two parts. The first has the same configurational form as the equilibrium weak-gravity free energy with gravity replaced by the effective gravity, and it determines the first-order configurational transition between the two separated liquid-gas arrangements. The second is a residual excess-latent-heat contribution that vanishes without heat conduction. Although it does not decide which separated liquid-gas arrangement is thermodynamically favored, this residual part is needed to derive the fundamental relation in the laboratory variables and to recover thermodynamic observables such as the spatially averaged pressure. The same residual contribution reshapes the barrier geometry, ridge/valley structure, and interfacial anomalies of the fixed-global-temperature free-energy landscape. Numerical examples based on the van der Waals model illustrate the resulting landscape structure, and estimates of experimental scales suggest a setup for detecting the effective-gravity inversion.
summary: This paper develops a global thermodynamic framework to analyze liquid-gas coexistence in fluids subjected to both heat conduction and weak gravity. It constructs a variational free-energy function, decomposing it to show that while the effective gravity dictates the primary phase ordering, a residual term is crucial for correctly calculating macroscopic thermodynamic observables. This work provides a general methodology for incorporating non-equilibrium driving forces into phase coexistence theory.
main_problem: The study investigates the global thermodynamics of liquid-gas coexistence in heat-conducting fluids subjected to weak gravity.
main_result: The free-energy landscape is decomposed into an effective-gravity part determining the primary phase ordering, and a residual excess-latent-heat contribution necessary for deriving fundamental thermodynamic relations and observables.
method: The authors employ a global variational free-energy function approach, extending equilibrium thermodynamics to incorporate non-equilibrium effects from heat conduction and gravity.
model_or_system: The system is a fluid undergoing liquid-gas coexistence in a container under weak gravity and imposed temperature gradients. The analysis uses the van der Waals model for numerical illustration.
key_observables: Spatially averaged pressure, global chemical potential, and the variational free-energy landscape structure.
important_parameters: Weak gravity ($mgL$), temperature difference ($\Xi$), and the dimensionless parameter $\varepsilon$ characterizing the weak coupling regime.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes a sharp interface and operates in the linear response regime, neglecting explicit interfacial transport processes.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate variational free-energy landscapes in $(\xi_1, \xi_2)$ space, showing how temperature differences ($\Xi$) deform the barrier shape and how the ordering changes across different physical regimes.
paper_structure: The paper builds from setting up the global thermodynamic framework, deriving steady-state conditions via variational minimization, decomposing the free energy into effective-gravity and residual parts, and finally analyzing the resulting free-energy landscape structure.
why_interesting: The rigorous application of global thermodynamics to non-equilibrium systems, particularly separating the ordering principle from residual non-equilibrium corrections, provides a powerful theoretical template for analyzing complex phase transitions in open quantum or classical systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 (a) System setup under gravity and heat flow. The lower and upper coarse-grained regions are separated by the formal dividing position x = l and are characterized by (N ℓ, V ℓ) and (Nu, V u). In a heterogeneous state, the actual liquid-gas interface is at x = xθ. (b) The three configuration classes: (L, G), (G, L), and homogeneous. In the homogeneous panel, the dashed line indicates only the formal partition, not a macroscopic interface.

Fig. 2 Interfacial metastable patterns for T2 > T1. In the lower-free-energy (L, G) branch for geff > 0, θ < Tc(pθ) and the gas adjacent to the interface is supercooled. In the lower-free-energy (G, L) branch for geff < 0, θ > Tc(pθ) and the liquid adjacent to the interface is superheated. The green shaded layers are schematic; reversing the heat-flow direction exchanges the two patterns.

Fig. 3 Configurational transition reproduced and adapted from Figs. 2(a), 2(b), 3(a), and 3(b) of Ref. [17] under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International license. (a) Center-of-mass displacement under fixed gravity. (b) Representative configurations; light-green regions indicate interfacial metastable layers. (c) Cusp of Fg at geff = 0. (d) Constant-mgeffL lines in the (mgL, Ξ) plane.

Figure 3 summarizes the configurational-transition scenario of Ref. [17], now expressed using the stationary values derived above and the equilibrium gravity formulation of Ref. [16]. Figure 3(a) plots the stationary center-of-mass displacement obtained by applying (3.19) with g replaced by geff. Fig- ure 3(b) displays the corresponding phase configurations, using the ordering in (5.15) and the interfacial metastable patterns classified by (5.23) and Fig. 2. The labels (1)–(3) mark the same representative states in panels (a) and (b): state (1) is the minimizing (G, L) configuration, whereas states (2) and (3) are minimizing (L, G) configurations. Fig. 4 Schematic comparison of the chemical-potential profile in (a) equilibrium under gravity and (b) a heat-conducting steady state under gravity. (a) In equilibrium, µ(x) is continuous and linear in x with slope −mg. (b) In heat conduction, shown for (ℓ, u) = (L, G) and Ξ > 0, µ(x) is generally discontinuous at xθ because θ ̸= Tc(pθ). The dashed line indicates µc(θ), and the blue line schematically represents the global chemical potential ˜µg.
